risk_of_bias.support_text: Participants were assigned to treatment groups using stratified randomisation. Stratified variables were gender, enrolment in CR, and whether or not the subject required instructions in Spanish.
The study biostatistician concealed the sequence of allocation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: The investigators, medical providers, CR providers, and data collection staff were all blinded to treatment status. No statement regarding participants or TM teachers. We have left as unclear risk as it is difficult, if not impossible, to blind participants and personnel to lifestyle interventions.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment (detection bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Data collection staff were all blinded to treatment status. Endpoint data were collected at the field site in New York and coded without patient or treatment status identifiers.

risk_of_bias.domain: Incomplete outcome data (attrition bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Of the 56 participants, 53 completed baseline myocardial blood flow measurements, 43 completed the 12-week post-test for resting myocardial blood flow, and 37 completed baseline and post-test MFR assessment.
No protocol or trial registry number identified to check.

characteristics.participants:
Recruited from the outpatient cardiology services of New York Presbyterian Hospital ‐ Columbia University Medical Centre. Recruitment dates not reported.
Inclusion criteria:
African‐American men and women of any age with documented CHD (previous MI, CABG, PCI or chronic stable angina)
Exclusion criteria:
Concomitant life‐threatening illness or physical and mental disabilities that would interfere with study participation
Number eligible not reported; 19 randomised to the intervention group (mean age 62, 52.6% men) and 18 to the comparison group (mean age 63.7, 66.7% men)
Medications at baseline:
Statin therapy at baseline in 47.4% of the intervention group and 50% of the comparison group. Antihypertensive therapy at baseline in 84.2% of the intervention group and 100% of the comparison group. Aspirin therapy at baseline in 73.7% of the intervention group and 66.7% of the comparison group.
Medication change during the trial not reported
characteristics.interventions:
Intervention(duration 12 weeks, setting ‐ Columbia University Medical Centre):
TM plus cardiac rehabilitation or usual care
Certified and experienced TM teachers, similar in race and ethnicity to the patient population, delivered the intervention. The core instruction in the TM technique involved a 7‐step course over 6 days, complying with the standard format (Roth 2018). Each session lasted 1 to 1.5 hours. The general format of most sessions was lecture/discussion in both personal one‐on‐one and small‐group formats. Following core instruction in the TM programme, patients attended biweekly follow‐up sessions for the remainder of the 12‐week intervention period.
Comparison(duration 12 weeks, setting not reported)
Cardiac rehabilitation or usual care
characteristics.outcomes: Follow‐up at 12 weeks: BP, depression, perceived stress, lipids, smoking, BMI, HRQoL
characteristics.notes:
Country:USA
Funding:This study was supported by a Grant from NIH ‐ National Heart, Lung and Blood Institute, # HL 100386.
Declarations of interest:All the authors report that there are no relevant financial conflicts of interest to disclose.

sections.text:
National practice guidelines recommend cardiac rehabilitation (CR) for patients with coronary heart disease (CHD) (1). However, these guidelines consistently point out the evidence for CR in the secondary prevention of CHD in minorities, especially African Americans is lacking (2, 3).

The core component of cardiac rehabilitation (CR) is aerobic exercise with medical management of CVD risk factors added (4). However, decades of epidemiologic research demonstrates that psychosocial stress increases the risk for CHD (5, 6). These high rates of psychosocial and environmental stress are implicated in the disproportionate rates of CHD morbidity and mortality in African Americans (7, 8).

A series of clinical trials reported that stress reduction with the Transcendental Meditation technique reduces CHD risk factors (9–12) surrogate markers of CHD (13), all cause and cardiovascular mortality (14), and the composite of mortality, myocardial infarction and stroke (15) in African Americans and whites. However, despite these findings, stress reduction therapies are not routinely included in CR programs for either the general population or high risk groups, such as African Americans (16, 17).

Recently, a study evaluated the effects of adding stress management training to exercise-based CR (17). The results suggested that stress management combined with CR improved rates of clinical outcomes over three years follow up, although there were no differences in CHD biomarkers between the groups (17).

Given this background, the overall objective of the current study was to explore the effects of four treatment conditions on a measure of CHD in African American patients. These conditions were: 1. stress reduction with the Transcendental Meditation (TM) technique, 2. Standard, exercise-based CR alone, 3. CR + TM and 4. usual care. In this study, CHD burden was assessed by myocardial flow reserve (MFR) using quantitative 13N-ammonia positron emission tomography (PET). MFR has been previously validated as a prognostic biomarker for CHD risk (18, 19) (20). The average sensitivity and specificity of PET myocardial perfusion imaging is approximately 90% (21, 22). The goal of this pilot study was to estimate effect sizes of these four treatment conditions and to apply the results to the design and calculation of power and sample size for follow up randomized controlled trial (30).

## Overall design

African American men and women with documented CHD (previous MI, CABG, PCI or chronic stable angina) were recruited from the outpatient cardiology services of New York Presbyterian hospital - Columbia University Medical Center. All patients gave written informed consent for study participation. The study was approved by both the Columbia University Medical Center and Maharishi University of Management institutional review boards. In addition, all patients obtained approval from their cardiologists to participate in the study. The Data Safety and Monitoring Board approved the protocol and monitored the study.

A total of 56 patients were recruited and allocated to treatment conditions in two phases. In phase I of the study, 32 patients were randomly assigned to CR with or without stress reduction with TM. In phase II, 24 patients were randomly assigned to stress reduction with TM or usual care alone.

The primary outcome was myocardial flow reserve (MFR). Myocardial blood flow was assessed by quantitative positron emission tomography (PET) using 13N-ammonia at rest and during pharmacologic stress with adenosine. Cardiac PET imaging was performed at baseline and after 12 weeks of intervention. Secondary outcomes of blood pressure, lipids, diet, exercise, smoking, and psychosocial factors were assessed at baseline, 6 weeks and 12 weeks. All subjects continued standard medical care provided by their usual health care providers at Columbia University Medical Center or in the community during the study.

## Subjects

The 56 participants in this study met CHD eligibility criteria defined by Medicare for phase II cardiac rehabilitation (23).

The inclusion criteria were: 1. gender: male or female 2. age: no limitations 3. CHD: documented by medical history of at least one of the following: a) acute myocardial infarction (MI) within the preceding 12 months, b. coronary artery bypass surgery (CABG), c) percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), d) chronic stable angina and 4. written informed consent

The exclusion criteria included concomitant life-threatening illness or physical and mental disabilities that would interfere with study participation.

Participants were assigned to treatment groups using stratified randomization. Stratification variables were gender, enrollment in CR, and whether or not the subject required instructions in Spanish. The study biostatistician concealed the sequence of allocation.

The investigators, medical providers, CR providers and data collection staff were all blinded to treatment status. Endpoint data was collected at the field site in New York and was coded without patient or treatment status identifiers.

## Outcomes

The demonstration and quantification of myocardial ischemia with nuclear imaging techniques is a well-established method to predict CHD clinical risk in cardiac patient populations (22). Myocardial ischemia is a powerful predictor of hard CHD endpoints - nonfatal myocardial infarction and death (24). Myocardial blood flow determined by quantitative PET is an independent predictor of the coronary circulatory dysfunction and CHD clinical events and shows superiority to conventional imaging methods (25, 26).

## PET Imaging Protocol

PET myocardial perfusion imaging was performed using 13N-ammonia for the measurement of myocardial blood flow at rest and during pharmacological stress using the vasodilator, adenosine. PET imaging was performed in a blinded procedure before and after 12 weeks of intervention in all subjects. The PET data were acquired on a Siemens Biograph Truepoint 40 PET/CT scanner (Siemens Medical Solutions USA, Inc., Hoffman Estates, IL). The Siemens biograph uses computed tomography (CT) acquisition for attenuation correction as well as co-registration with the emission image. PET data are collected in 47 planes 3.375 mm thick that cover a range of 16 cm. A dose of 10 mCi of 13N-ammonia was injected followed by a 10-min static, dynamic and dynamic acquisition. All scanning is performed in 3D list mode and reconstructed with filtered back projection using a Hann filter cutoff at 1 cycle/cm. Then, pharmacological stress imaging was performed on each subject. The rest and stress imaging protocols were identical except a 6-minute infusion of adenosine ([140 ug/kg/min) was started 2 minutes prior to the ammonia injection for the stress imaging session. The injections of ammonia were separated by at least 50 minutes to allow the first injection to decay to a level where it will not interfere with the stress study. A low-dose CT transmission scan (120 kV, 25 mA) was obtained for attenuation correction of PET data prior to rest and stress scans.

## PET Flow Measure

To calculate myocardial flow reserve, measurements of myocardial blood flow at rest and during adenosine stress were obtained. Corridor 4DM (INVIA, Ann Arbor, MI) was used for MFR measurement. MFR is the ratio of maximum flow during stress to flow at rest. An overall measure of MFR for the entire myocardium (across all the 17 regions) was calculated. Secondarily, regional flow reserve was calculated according to vascular territories designated by standard techniques, i.e., left anterior descending (LAD), left circumflex (LCX), and right coronary artery (RCA).

## Secondary Outcomes

### Blood Pressure

The procedure for determining clinic blood pressure measurements was a calibrated manual sphygmomanometer (27).

## Secondary Outcomes

### Lipids

After an overnight fast, plasma samples for total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, and VLDL were assayed by standard reference methods using a research laboratory that was CDC-certified.

## Secondary Outcomes

### Substance use: Smoking and Alcohol

Alcohol and smoking use were assessed by standard questionnaires that queried patients by the Weekly Recall (WR) method for number of drinks of alcohol consumed during the prior week or number of cigarettes smoked per day.

## Secondary Outcomes

### Psychosocial Risk Factors

The 21 item Beck Depression Inventory (BDI)-II measured presence of depression symptoms (28). The General Health Questionnaire (GHQ) is a brief 20-item questionnaire that was used to assess general emotional distress. The Perceived Social Support Scale (PSSS) is a 12-item self-report scale that assessed perceived availability and satisfaction with support received from family, friends, and other persons.

## Secondary Outcomes

### Demographics

At baseline, demographic variables such as age, gender, SES (education and income levels), occupation, marital status, and family history of cardiovascular disease were collected.

## Secondary Outcomes

### Demographics

#### Interventions

All patients continued standard medical care with by their usual health care providers during the course of the study.

## Cardiac Rehabilitation (CR) Program

The CR program included medical evaluation, prescribed exercise, cardiovascular risk factor modification, education and counseling. The program began with a baseline clinical examination and symptom-limited exercise test followed by supervised exercise sessions three times per week for 12 weeks. The American College of Sports Medicine guidelines were used for exercise prescription (29). The baseline exercise stress test was followed by 36 supervised exercise sessions. Each session lasted 45–60 minutes.

Monthly educational and social support sessions in the standard CR program were conducted in small groups. These sessions lasted 60–90 minutes each. Educational elements were based on guidelines for diet, weight, diabetes, lipids, blood pressure, smoking cessation, physical activity management recommended by current secondary prevention of CHD practice guidelines. Outcomes measures such as weight, waist circumference, cholesterol panels, blood glucose, blood pressure, and aerobic capacity were tracked on all patients. Patients with clinically significant levels of psychological distress were referred for care within the Columbia University Medical Center (CUMC) system. The education sessions were staffed by CUMC dieticians, nurses, health educators, exercise physiologists and physicians.

## Stress Reduction – Transcendental Meditation

The Transcendental Meditation (TM) technique was chosen from among a variety of available stress reduction practices because of its standardized and systematic teaching protocol, feasibility in this high-risk minority population, reproducible physiological results, and distinctive clinical effectiveness as reported in previous comparative studies, clinical controlled trials, and meta-analyses (12, 30, 31). The Transcendental Meditation technique is a traditional meditation modality that has its origin in the ancient Vedic tradition (32). Certified and experienced Transcendental Meditation teachers, similar in race and ethnicity to the patient population, delivered the treatment on site at CUMC.

The core instruction in the Transcendental Meditation technique involved a seven-step course over six days, which followed the standard format nationally and internationally (32, 33). Each session lasted 1–1.5 hours. The general format of most sessions was lecture/discussion in both personal one on one and small group formats.

Following core instruction in the Transcendental Meditation program, patients attended biweekly follow-up sessions for the remainder of the 12-week intervention period. There were five additional sessions in this maintenance phase consisting of: a) verification of correct practice of the Transcendental Meditation technique; b) discussion of personal experiences; and c) additional information on the physiological, psychological, and behavioral health effects of the program.

## Stress Reduction – Transcendental Meditation

### Statistical Analysis

In accordance with contemporary recommendations for analysis of pilot studies with small sizes, the results were analyzed by descriptive statistics using standardized differences or effect size (34). Change from baseline to 12 weeks in outcomes were analyzed for effect size. The effect size was calculated using Hedges’ bias-corrected formula for small samples, based on the mean change on the outcome, divided by the standard deviation of the baseline scores (35). The grouping factors were TM vs no TM and CR vs no CR. An effect size for each outcome variable was calculated based on the the standardized difference in changes in the combined TM vs combined no TM groups.

Of the 56 patients who were recruited and assigned to treatment, 53 patients completed baseline myocardial blood flow measurement, 43 patients completed the 12-week posttest for resting myocardial blood flow and 37 patients completed baseline and posttest MFR assessment (66%). Table 1 shows the baseline demographic and biomedical characteristics of the 43 subjects who completed testing. In table 1, for simplicity and ease of presentation, the four intervention groups were collapsed into two groups, i.e., groups with and without TM instruction.

For compliance with interventions, we collected data on attendance at instructional meetings. Attendance for the cardiac rehabilitation sessions was 63% for the TM + CR group and 58% for the CR alone group. Among patients assigned to the TM group, 71% completed instruction in the TM technique. Meeting attendance for the TM classes was 58%.

Table 2 and Figures 1 and 2 show the main effects on myocardial flow reserve (MFR) and their effect sizes in the four main groups (CR, CR+TM, TM, usual care) and the two combined groups (TM combined and no TM combined). As shown in Table 2, the largest effect (both percentage change and effect size) was seen in the TM plus cardiac rehabilitation (CR+ TM) group (+20.7%; ES= 0.64) compared to the CR alone group (+5.8%; ES= 0.17) although this difference was smaller after adjusting for rate pressure product (RPP). Of the four groups, the CR group alone (+5.8%; ES= 0.17) and usual care group (−10.3%; ES= −0.31) showed the smallest MFR changes and effect sizes both unadjusted and adjusted for RPP (table 2).

Table 2 shows the change from baseline to posttest after 12 weeks of intervention for the combined TM groups (CR + TM and TM alone) versus combined No TM groups (CR alone and usual care). Global myocardial flow reserve increased 14% in the TM combined group ES=0.56, both adjusting and not adjusting for RPP), but remained virtually unchanged (−2.0%) in the No TM group (ES=−0.08 unadjusted, ES=0.05 adjusted for RPP). These differences between the combined TM and No TM groups were not significant (p=.23 unadjusted for RPP, two-tailed; p=.29 adjusted for RPP, two-tailed).

Table 3 shows the baseline and posttest PET myocardial blood flow (MBF) values used to calculate the MFR values for each treatment group. Table 4 shows changes in MFR for the three major vascular territories and global changes. Overall, there was a 0.36 or 36% global increase in MFR that was especially evident in the LCX and RCA regions. Figure 3 shows the color PET imaged coronary flow at baseline (3a) and at six-month posttest (3b) for a patient in the CR + TM group along with the quantified MFR values globally and regionally.

Table 5 shows changes in secondary outcomes for the combined TM group compared to no TM group. As can been seen, there were reductions in total cholesterol, LDL, triglycerides, smoking, depression and stress (General Health questionnaire for negative affect) in the combined TM group compared to no TM. Blood pressure showed modest decreases in the non-TM group and modest increases in the TM group.

This pilot study explored the effects of cardiac rehabilitation (CR) with and without a meditation component on myocardial flow reserve assessed by quantitative 13N-ammonia PET in African American patients with CHD. Findings suggested substantial improvements in MFR over the 12-week intervention period for the CR + meditation group of 21%, the meditation group alone of 13%, and the combined TM group (TM with and without CR) of 14%. By comparison, the CR group alone, the usual care group and the combined No TM group showed MFR changes of +6%, −10% and −2% respectively. The increased MFR in the combined meditation group was observed across all three vascular regions of the myocardium, especially LCX and RCA regions compared to no change or small decrease for the combined No TM group.

This study benefited from the use of myocardial perfusion imaging with quantitative 13N-ammonia PET. In recent years, PET myocardial perfusion imaging with MFR has demonstrated an exponential increase in the evidence base for its diagnostic and prognostic value (36). Schindler et al report that functional alterations in coronary circulation measured by PET imaging appear to be prognostically superior to structural assessments of CHD (i.e. coronary angiography, calcification) in the prediction of future CHD clinical events (25). PET measurement of myocardial flow reserve provides a sensitive index for evaluating changes in CHD severity with intervention (26). Using non-invasive PET assessment, MFR has been found to be a strong independent predictor of cardiac mortality (37, 38). These studies of CHD patients found an average sensitivity and specificity of approximately 90% using PET myocardial perfusion imaging. (21, 22). Several studies have documented the incremental prognostic value of PET-derived MFR over perfusion defects size and severity and clinical factors for predicting CHD clinical outcomes (39)

There have been a few other lifestyle modification studies employing myocardial perfusion imaging as the outcome. Sdringola et al found that improvements in myocardial perfusion imaging with PET correlated with intensity of medical therapy and predicted clinical outcomes (40). Two studies of exercise training over 6 and 14 weeks respectively reported improvements in PET determined MFR by approximately 20% (41, 42). This is somewhat similar to result that we found with CR alone. and less than with CR + meditation.

In a trial of intensive lifestyle modification, including diet, exercise, social support and stress management, Gould et al randomized patients with CHD to active lifestyle intervention or control (43). After five years of intervention, the severity, size and of perfusion defects on stress PET improved in experimental patients and worsened in controls. The authors concluded that PET myocardial imaging appeared more sensitive than quantitative coronary angiography in detecting changes in CAD in response to intervention.

In a partially comparable study, Blumenthal and colleagues published a trial of 151 outpatients with CHD who were randomized to 12 weeks of conventional cardiac rehab or cardiac rehabilitation plus stress management training (17). The results showed that both groups exhibited similar reductions in CHD biomarkers. However, the stress management + CR group showed greater reductions in psychological stress and lower rates of clinical events compared to CR alone and a matched group of usual care patients. While there were parallels in study design between this study and our present one, a major difference is the apparent improvement in myocardial perfusion of a validated measure of CHD with TM compared to no change in biomarkers with stress management training. Whether or not the present subjects will show differences in clinical events remains to be determined.

The finding of improved myocardial flow reserve in this study are corroborated by earlier studies on meditation. Zamarra, et al showed marked decreases in exercise-induced myocardial ischemia in CAD patients who practiced the TM program over an eight-month intervention period compared to usual care controls (41). These patients showed significant pre-posttest increases in exercise tolerance, maximum work load, delay of onset of ST segment depression and a significant decrease in double product (44). A randomized controlled trial of high CVD risk African Americans assigned to TM or health education assessed changes in atherosclerotic burden by carotid intima medial thickness (13). Significant decreases in IMT were observed in the TM group after 6–9 months compared to the health education control. A high correlation has been reported between stenosed coronary and carotid arteries (39, 45, 46). In terms of CVD clinical events, a pooled study of two randomized trials of African Americans and whites reported 20–30% reductions on all-cause and CVD mortality in subjects practicing the TM program over eight years (14). A randomized controlled trial of 201 African American CHD patients randomized to TM or health education control showed 48% reduction in the composite of all cause mortality, nonfatal MI and nonfatal stroke in the meditation group (15).

There are at least two possible mechanisms by which meditation alone or in combination with standard, exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation improved MFR in this study. The first is anatomic regression of localized coronary artery stenosis. However, given the relatively short intervention period of 12 weeks, a second, physiological mechanism is more likely. That is, improved endothelial-mediated coronary artery and arteriolar vasomotor function. This has been previously shown to occur within weeks to months after vigorous risk factor modification before structural regression occurs (25, 47).

The improvements in myocardial flow reserve in the combined meditation group over 12 weeks of intervention was associated with reductions in psychosocial stress as indicated moderate effects sizes for change in depression and negative affect. There were also small reductions in cigarette use, total cholesterol and LDL-cholesterol and triglycerides in the TM group compared to controls These changes either together or separately may have contributed to short-term improvements in myocardial blood flow. The changes in blood pressure between the meditation and non-meditation groups were contrary to findings in previous trials of TM and high BP (10, 48) and are perhaps related to lack of high BP at baseline and small sample size, and deserve further exploration.

## Study Limitations

The principal limitation of this study is the relatively small sample size. Particularly with the four-cell design (i.e. (TM +CR, TM, CR, UC) plus the combined groups, samples were limited to 9–19 subjects per group. However, in accordance with recent statistical guidelines, the study design did allow us to calculate MFR change and associated effect sizes, the appropriate descriptive statistic for this type of study (30). The effect sizes are consistent with other studies of more standard interventions in CHD (16). These effect sizes give information on the magnitude of the intervention effects and are invaluable in estimating efficacy for designing future experimental studies of cardiac rehabilitation with and without meditation. As Diamond and Kaul highlight, measures of effect size may communicate the clinical importance of study results better than conventional assessments of statistical significance (49).

Another limitation of the current study is its generalizability to other racial and ethnic groups. While the sample was exclusively African American, other studies on Transcendental Meditation in diverse populations have shown improvement in markers of CHD that corroborate the present findings (11, 14, 44). Furthermore, the clinical outcome results of the Blumenthal et al trial of stress management training combined with CR in diverse patients are consistent with the present study (17). In the multimodality intervention of Gould, Ornish et al in diverse patients, quantitative PET was used although not MFR (43). Nevertheless, the results are generally consistent with the present study even though the follow-up time of the present study was limited to the standard 12-week duration of CR and was not sufficient to follow up for clinical events. A potential limitation may be a difference in baseline resting MBF between the two groups. However, subjects were randomly allocated to treatment groups and MFR which was the primary outcome was similar between the groups at baseline.

These pilot study on the addition of stress-reducing Transcendental Meditation to standard cardiac rehabilitation provides key data for estimating clinical treatment effects and designing future trials of CR with and without meditation. These results suggest the utility of adding an effective means of stress reduction such as the TM technique to conventional CR in the secondary prevention of CHD and merit more definitive evaluation in future randomized controlled trials.

## New Knowledge Gained:

For patients with known coronary heart disease, standard exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation may be enhanced by adding meditation as an integral stress reducing component. The results of improved myocardial blood reserve may improve quality of life and contribute to the prevention of progression of CHD and recurrent coronary events.

The authors are grateful to Magnolia Jimenez for her data collection and administrative contributions, Laura Alcorn for data management, Linda Heaton and Carol Jarvis for administrative and technical support. Ines Spinal, Claudia Rodriguez and Josh Pittman taught the Transcendental Meditation program.

## Source of Funding:

This study was supported by a grant from NIH - National Heart, Lung and Blood Institute, # HL 100386

review_title: Meditation for the primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular disease
